seasonal_wine.name: Sicilia en Primeur, spring
seasonal_wine.description: Assovini Sicilia gathers international wine press and buyers in Palermo for two days of vertical and horizontal tastings of the new vintage from over 100 Sicilian producers. The format mirrors Bordeaux's en primeur, with producers releasing barrel samples and current-release wines simultaneously. Assovini uses the event to announce the vintage character assessment for the island.
seasonal_wine.tip: Trade and press credentials required; the walk-around tasting on day two is the best opportunity to compare across appellations in a single session.

seasonal_wine.name: Contrade dell'Etna producer tasting, spring
seasonal_wine.description: The Contrade dell'Etna gathering brings together producers from the named contrade (individual lava-flow micro-zones) of Etna DOC for a walk-around tasting typically held on the volcano itself or in a venue in Randazzo. The event is the primary opportunity to taste side-by-side from Solicchiata, Calderara, Guardiola, Caselle, Santo Spirito and other named contrade in the same session.
seasonal_wine.tip: This event is small and invitation-heavy; follow the Etna Wine Festival and producer social media for ticket availability, which often appears two to three weeks before the event date.

seasonal_wine.name: Cantine Aperte, late May
seasonal_wine.description: Movimento Turismo del Vino's national Cantine Aperte (Open Cellars) day is observed across Sicily with dozens of producer estates opening their doors for walk-in tastings, cellar tours and vineyard picnics. In Sicily it is particularly active in Marsala, the Etna DOC, the Vittoria area and the Menfi coast. Producers not normally open for walk-in visits offer this day as a rare point of open access.
seasonal_wine.tip: Plan routes in advance: Etna, Vittoria and Marsala are each a long drive from each other. Choose one sub-zone and do it thoroughly rather than trying to cover the island in a day.

seasonal_wine.name: Etna harvest, September to October
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, September to mid-October depending on altitude
seasonal_wine.description: The Etna harvest is one of the most dramatic in Italy. At 700 to 1000 metres above sea level, Nerello Mascalese ripens slowly: the north-facing Solicchiata and Calderara contrade are typically the last to be picked, often in mid-October. Harvest-experience visits are available at estates including Cornelissen, Passopisaro and Terre Nere, where guests participate in hand-harvest of the pre-phylloxera alberello (bush vine) plots.
what_to_taste: Etna Rosso DOC just-pressed free-run juice
seasonal_wine.tip: Altitude on Etna means a temperature differential of 8-10 C between the coast and the harvest sites. Pack layers even in September.

seasonal_wine.name: Nero d'Avola harvest in Noto, August
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, mid August to early September
seasonal_wine.description: In the Val di Noto in the far south-east of Sicily, Nero d'Avola ripens in intense summer heat to very high sugar levels. The harvest typically begins in mid-August, among the earliest in Italy. Arianna Occhipinti's estate in Vittoria and Gulfi's holdings in Chiaramonte Gulfi hold harvest-open days during this period, offering a contrast to the cool-site Etna harvest eight weeks later.
what_to_taste: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG Frappato component
seasonal_wine.tip: August heat in the Val di Noto is extreme; the early-morning hours of the harvest are the practical time to visit. Bring factor 50.

seasonal_wine.name: Calici di Stelle, August
seasonal_wine.description: Calici di Stelle is a national Italian wine event held on the night of San Lorenzo (10 August), when the Perseids meteor shower peaks. Across Sicily, participating wine towns open piazzas, city walls and hilltop esplanades for evening outdoor tastings under the stars. In Sicily the event is active in Marsala, Ragusa, Noto and Taormina, with local producers pouring by the glass against a backdrop of meteor-watching.
seasonal_wine.tip: The event in Marsala typically focuses on fortified and oxidative styles that suit warm evening drinking; Noto leans into Nero d'Avola and Moscato di Noto.

seasonal_wine.name: ViniMilo, Milo (Etna Bianco Superiore), late summer
seasonal_wine.description: ViniMilo is a small annual wine fair held in Milo, the village at the eastern foot of Etna that is the only commune entitled to the Etna Bianco Superiore DOC designation. Milo's Carricante-based whites are among the most mineral and age-worthy in southern Italy. The fair typically runs over a long weekend and brings together producers from the entire Etna DOC alongside food stalls specialising in Etna-grown produce.
seasonal_wine.tip: Milo is on the quieter, cooler eastern face of Etna. The village itself is tiny; book accommodation in Zafferana Etnea below and drive up.

seasonal_wine.name: Marsala vendemmia and Florio harvest events, late September
seasonal_wine.description: The Marsala harvest in the Trapani province typically takes place in late September. Cantine Florio and Pellegrino open cellar doors during the harvest period for guided tours of the historic bagli (warehouse cellars) where new-vintage base wine begins its Marsala solera process alongside decades-old reserves. The combination of active fermentation and aged-oxidative notes makes harvest visits to Marsala producers uniquely sensory.
seasonal_wine.tip: The Florio bagli on the Marsala waterfront contains reserves going back over a century; ask the guide for a comparison pour from a 20-year-old Vergine Stravecchio.

seasonal_wine.name: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG new vintage release, spring
seasonal_wine.description: The Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG annata is typically released in the spring following harvest, after a minimum of 18 months in vessel. The Classico tier requires 24 months. COS, Arianna Occhipinti and Valle dell'Acate release their new vintages during this window, often with a brief open-cellar day. The release is the ideal moment to taste the current-vintage Frappato-Nero d'Avola blend alongside older vintages showing the DOCG's development arc.
seasonal_wine.tip: Ask producers for a side-by-side of the current vintage and a five-year-old from the same producer: Cerasuolo di Vittoria ages better than most domestic buyers expect.

seasonal_wine.name: Pantelleria Zibibbo harvest, August
seasonal_wine.description: On Pantelleria, the Zibibbo harvest takes place in high summer under extreme heat. After picking, grapes are laid on mats or hung in shaded passiti sheds for weeks to concentrate sugar before pressing. Donnafugata, Abraxas and Murana all hold visits during the passiti drying period, showing the Zibibbo at its most aromatic stage before fermentation. The resulting Passito di Pantelleria typically releases the following spring.
what_to_taste: Zibibbo passiti in the drying stage
Passito di Pantelleria DOC previous vintage
seasonal_wine.tip: Ferry connections to Pantelleria from Trapani are frequent in summer but booked out weeks in advance; flights from Palermo and Catania are the faster option.

seasonal_wine.name: Malvasia delle Lipari harvest on Salina, September
seasonal_wine.description: On the Aeolian island of Salina, the Malvasia delle Lipari DOC harvest takes place in September. The aromatic Malvasia di Lipari grape is harvested by hand from terraced vineyards overlooking the sea and dried in the sun on cane mats (appassimento) to produce the amber, honeyed passito wine. Carlo Hauner and Barone di Villagrande are the leading producers. The ferry crossing from Milazzo and the island's volcanic landscape make this a memorable wine-tourism experience.
what_to_taste: local Aeolian capers and cured fish pairing
seasonal_wine.tip: Salina's Malvasia dessert wine is one of Italy's most underrated island wines; the naturale dry version is rarely exported and worth seeking at the cellar door.

seasonal_wine.name: Marsala solera season, winter cellar visits
seasonal_wine.description: The quiet winter months are the best time to visit the historic Marsala bagli. With no harvest activity and cooler temperatures favouring oxidative clarity, winter tastings at Pellegrino, Florio and Marco de Bartoli are unhurried and focused on the differentiated Marsala styles: Fine, Superiore, Superiore Riserva, Vergine and Vergine Stravecchio. Tasting Vergine against Fine shows the full range of complexity the appellation can achieve.
seasonal_wine.tip: Marco de Bartoli's Vecchio Samperi is technically not a Marsala (it is unfortified), but no winter Marsala tour is complete without tasting it; book a visit on the Petrosino estate.
